Open-Source-First Stack Recommendation ### VERIFIED Open WebUI is self-hosted and offline-capable, and it supports Ollama and OpenAI-compatible protocols. AnythingLLM offers a developer API and self-hosted persistence. Mem0 offers open-source self-hosting and local Ollama-based operation. Flowise supports self-hosting, workspaces, API/CLI/SDK, and warns that self-hosting requires technical skill. n8n explicitly positions self-hosting as suitable for technically capable operators and also offers a self-hosted AI starter kit. Supabase supports self-hosting with Docker. ([Open WebUI](https://docs.openwebui.com/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) ### Tool-by-tool role **Open WebUI** Best as the main self-hosted interaction layer. Strong fit as the visible “hub” for SacredSpace. Good for local and remote models, prompt management, and controlled user-facing workspaces. ([Open WebUI](https://docs.openwebui.com/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **AnythingLLM** Best as a pragmatic no-code RAG/workspace layer. Good if you want per-workspace document context fast. Less ideal as the governing control plane. ([AnythingLLM](https://docs.useanything.com/features/api?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **Mem0** Best as structured memory infrastructure, especially if you want portable agent memory separated from chat UI. Very strong for “memory as service.” ([Mem0](https://docs.mem0.ai/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **Ollama** Best local model runtime anchor. Simple local API, easy WSL2/Windows story, strong fit for sovereign fallback. ([Ollama Docs](https://docs.ollama.com/api/introduction?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **LangGraph** Best orchestration layer for deterministic agent flows, retries, human review, and durable execution. Best fit for SacredSpace sync/agent governance logic. ([LangChain](https://langchain-ai.github.io/langgraph/tutorials/usaco/usaco/?ref=blog.langchain.dev&utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **Flowise** Useful if you want visual workflow building and team workspaces, but it increases surface area. Better as optional layer than core spine for a solo operator. ([docs.flowiseai.com](https://docs.flowiseai.com/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **n8n** Excellent for automation around notifications, backups, packet distribution, scheduled audits, and connector glue. Not ideal as the main AI operating layer, but strong as automation fabric. ([n8n Docs](https://docs.n8n.io/hosting/?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **Supabase** Use only if you genuinely need relational state, auth, storage, or a shared SQL ledger. Overkill for phase 1. ([Supabase](https://supabase.com/docs/guides/self-hosting?utm_source=chatgpt.com)) **LibreChat** Still useful, but for your specific goal I would put it behind Open WebUI unless you specifically want its assistant/workflow ergonomics. I would not run both at the center at first. **ChromaDB** Good if you need explicit vector DB control, but Mem0/AnythingLLM may already cover the first practical need. **MCP-compatible servers** Useful for tool exposure and context bridges, but they should be **augmenters**, not your first governance substrate. ### Recommended stacks #### 1. Minimal stack ```text Obsidian + YAML + Python sync/audit + Ollama + Open WebUI ``` Why this wins: - lowest operational burden - strong sovereignty - enough programmability - good WSL2/Docker fit - affordable API fallback model #### 2. Hybrid stack ```text Obsidian + YAML + Python sync/audit + Open WebUI + Ollama + LangGraph + optional Mem0 + OpenAI / Gemini / Anthropic APIs + proprietary UI mirrors ``` This is the **best fit** for you. #### 3. Full sovereign stack ```text Obsidian + YAML + Python sync/audit + Open WebUI + Ollama + LangGraph + Mem0 + self-hosted Supabase + n8n + optional local vector DB ``` This is powerful, but heavier than you need initially. ### My recommendation Use the **Hybrid stack** as your real target. --- ## 10. Python  from typing import Dict, List  HEBREW_VALUES = {     "א": 1, "ב": 2, "ג": 3, "ד": 4, "ה": 5, "ו": 6, "ז": 7, "ח": 8, "ט": 9,     "י": 10, "כ": 20, "ך": 20, "ל": 30, "מ": 40, "ם": 40, "נ": 50, "ן": 50,     "ס": 60, "ע": 70, "פ": 80, "ף": 80, "צ": 90, "ץ": 90,     "ק": 100, "ר": 200, "ש": 300, "ת": 400 }  GREEK_VALUES = {     "α": 1, "β": 2, "γ": 3, "δ": 4, "ε": 5, "ζ": 7, "η": 8, "θ": 9,     "ι": 10, "κ": 20, "λ": 30, "μ": 40, "ν": 50, "ξ": 60, "ο": 70, "π": 80,     "ρ": 100, "σ": 200, "ς": 200, "τ": 300, "υ": 400, "φ": 500, "χ": 600, "ψ": 700, "ω": 800 }  ENGLISH_VALUES = {chr(i + 96): i for i in range(1, 27)}  def detect_language(text: str) -> str:     if any("\u0590" <= c <= "\u05FF" for c in text): return "hebrew"     if any("\u0370" <= c <= "\u03FF" for c in text): return "greek"     return "english"  def calculate_gematria(text: str, system: str = "auto") -> Dict:     text = text.strip().lower()     if system == "auto": system = detect_language(text)          mapping = HEBREW_VALUES if system == "hebrew" else GREEK_VALUES if system == "greek" else ENGLISH_VALUES     breakdown = [{"letter": c, "value": mapping.get(c, 0)} for c in text if c in mapping or not c.strip()]     total = sum(item["value"] for item in breakdown)          return {         "word": text,         "system": system,         "total": total,         "breakdown": breakdown,         "katan": sum(int(d) for d in str(total)) if total > 9 else total     }  2.
